item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: is amazing.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The second and third images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the second image come from this National Geographic video with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons, and the third image's original source cannot be accessed with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, these images have been removed. The first image comes from here under Public Domain and is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a boitatá, alright. The problem is it isn't a particularly interesting one. Tone falls way off sometimes too ("a bastardized version of").

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is that Boitata?

Loved the concept, and the fact it is possibly a Brazilian critter just won me over

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This thing is poorly written, I suggest a rework to fix the language and some changes in tone. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amerindian is the preferred term in a number of areas, notably in this case Argentina and parts of Brazil. It is also formally used as the descriptor for the language groups of the Americas.

"Bastardized" also has a specific if unpleasant linguistic meaning, referring to a type of rough, highly deviant approximation of a language. "No speake espanisho" would be a bastardization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty decent writing and concept, but the uses of "Amerindian" ("indigenous", "native", or the specific culture would sound better) and "bastardized" ("pidgin", "creole", or simply "broken" depending on what you're going for) are very unscientific and somewhat racist for an article written in the 21st century. Downvoting until those are fixed, because they really throw the whole thing off.

Also, "Morse code" would be used in place of just "Morse".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Issues with tone, downvoted. Will likely provide in depth criticism when I get home, if I can remember.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, given the religious traditions of many of the region's ancient cultures, the ritual probably involves an obsidian dagger and a major artery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… The detainee's brother gonna masturbate in the hole or something? To give the womb the seed?
Damn that's….

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…you made that one Mario enemy an SCP.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why was the detainee vaccinated for most common diseases? Is it standard procedure for the Foundation to vaccinate people they detain, and if so, why include it in the report? Or is there some sort of biohazard around 2129-2 that gives the need for these vaccines?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…It doesn't explode when there's no oxygen.

Alright, I feel like an idiot. The cooling system could be a useful backup, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2129's containment chamber is a closed system. If you introduce 2 gigajouls per explosion, every two seconds, to a closed system, that's the equivalent of burning a literal ton of coal every minute.

Any closed system that you burn a ton of coal in will heat up you will very quickly reach the kind of temperatures that melt tungsten. Pulling some ballpark numbers from my industrial processing textbook…

Assuming the chamber is above ground and/or well ventilated with reasonable convection values, the outside of a 10 by 10 by 10 meter chamber that outputs 1 gigajoule per second (the amount of energy the explosions make) would be about 1500 K. And that's just the outside. The inside of the chamber would be much hotter. Hot enough to decompose concrete on prolonged exposure, to say nothing of the monitoring equipment in the chamber.

So the containment chamber needs a heavy-duty cooling system. You could hook it up to an industrial size ground-source water pump, or even turn it into a powerplant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It might just be me, but I found SCP-2191 to be far more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like a lot of things about this one. The gradual backstory implications, the oblique metaphors, "Procedure Perkele" (not gonna lie, I giggled — IIRC, that's basically Finnish for "oh fuck", and thus entirely too perfect for a containment-breach procedure involving multiple tons of high explosive)… However, there's a lot of weird, awkward phrasing. May I take a whack at cleaning it up?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We're not sure if it has a real physical body, so we would have to lure it into the water with more high explosives, which would risk massive detonation. It might fly out of the box we put it in, or detonate just before we put it in the box. Its also extremely fast, sentient, capable of flying and highly explosive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just curious regarding the containment of this thing. Wouldn't it be possible to just keep in it a vat of liquid that has no gaseous oxygen? Even placing it in a tub of water should put it in its dormant state, no? And if it's able to use any form of atomic oxygen to create a detonation, keeping in in a vat of mercury would be a way to indefinitely make it dormant.

If this is the case, this skip can be almost entirely neutralized by placing it in a box (provided that bax was filled with mercury). Does this not constitute a "safe" SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the first sentence of the description, but it was downhill from there in either direction, in terms of writing quality and the fulfillment of the good core idea. Overall impression makes me visualize something out of Garry's Mod.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the updates you've made on this, so now it has my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love the base idea, but the execution needs work- no vote for now. One of the main problems here for me are the containment procedures. I'm not opposed to detailed, complicated containment procedures myself, but you spend a lot of words repeating yourself and describing things in roundabout ways, and there are some things that have much simpler, cheaper solutions.

The oxygen systems, for example, can be described simply by saying that its containment chamber, and anything else you put it in, requires "a self-contained oxygen regulation system".

The real world version of your oxygen removal system is an "inert gas fire suppression system"- these can typically cycle the air for even moderately large chambers in seconds, by forcing the existing air mixture out with higher pressure xenon, which doesn't combust and is denser than oxygen (thus forcing it out into your air exchangers). Standard issue for modern chemical plants and archives. Simply pulling a vacuum, on the other hand, can cause a violent backdraft if you have even a minor air leak.

Tungsten is not a bad choice in this situation, but rather than making the whole wall out of it, use plates a couple centimeters thick backed with a few feet of concrete. Its a lot harder to melt, highly blast resistant if you reinforce it with steel mesh or carbon fiber, dirt cheap, and entirely plausible. Its what they build bunkers out of for a reason, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The difference is SCP-447's containment procedures can be summarized into "Keep it awy from dead bodies and your fine." This one isn't as easy to summarize. This one requires constantly deoxygenating its chamber and if there's even a minor equipment failure here, we have explosions everywhere and an extremely demanding recontainment procedure. If there is a small equipment failure for 447 then as long as the Foundation isn't leaving dead bodies around the worst that happens is the floor needs to be cleaned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The new interview is nice. However, the picture doesn't seem to be loading for SCP-2129-1, and I don't know what that is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So does SCP-447, but it's a safe class. Keter usually means that it can't be contained by normal methods, if I'm not mistaken. Normal by the foundation's standards, that is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll give it an Upvote because i do like the idea i think that an interview log could be cool and the titanium bugs me a bit as well still a pretty good idea and way better without the cross references to other SCP's

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually rather like this. The tone is meh and there are some grammatical issues, but the image of a giant explosive serpent flying around is kinda cool. Upvote of encouragement, keep writing.

item.title: Titanium=/= always good
item.content:encoded: So, I've been doing some thinking and research, and apparently tungsten has both higher heat resistance and strength. You should probably change that in the containment procedures. Sure, titanium is cheaper, but cost shouldn't be a big problem for the foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't this be Keter? It seems like it has fairly strict and lengthy containment procedures that could result in disaster if not followed to the letter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really really like this idea. I never read the original addendum, but definitely consider adding some creature dialogue (perhaps Portuguese + translation) or an interview as stated above.
Also, I would love to see an actual thermal photograph of this thing; it would definitely improve if not completely replace the need for a written description :)

EDIT: Awesome pic, you got a +1 from me, buddy. Well done :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely remove the addendum.

Also, compare to SCP-457. It's different enough, but is it interesting enough to have it separate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Get rid of the addendum. Also, an interview log might be nice to add some personality to the character, because right now it seems kind of boring and the story doesn't really go anywhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept but I feel that more could be done with it (interview logs, etc.) Also, like the others said, you should remove the addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The addendum with the references to 076 and 682 and saying that personnel will be penalized if they does not follow orders is something that was common years ago, but it became really unpopular now as the community grow tired of it. I recommend to remove it entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, i dont wanna downvote it, because its not bad, but i dont really wanna upvote it either.
meh. upvote cause more people should see it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty good idea, but formatting seems off.
The lolfoundation would have made me downvote, but it made me smirk, so it's a hesitant upvote for now.

item.title: Not bad, but it needs some work
item.content:encoded: It is not a bad idea, but it needs some work, there are some formatting issues and redundant phrasing, not necessarily a terrible thing, but it isn't helping your article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the base idea, but the last addendum is Lolfoundation, and there are formatting issues. Novote for now.
Edit: wasn't there an interview log before, which seemed pretty good? what happenned?
